column6: description   (WRASM dialect — documented from our own source)

column5: invoke — call a function with Win64 marshaling
column6: Calls `Func` with the x64 calling convention generated for you: arguments go to `rcx`, `rdx`, `r8`, `r9` then the stack, 32 bytes of shadow space are reserved, and the stack stays 16-byte aligned. The signature comes from the Windows knowledge base. **`invoke` uses `rax`/`eax` as scratch to stage stack arguments — never pass an argument that lives in `rax`; route it through memory or another register. Float arguments need a `real4`/`real8` annotation (e.g. `invoke f, real8 [rip+x]`) so they marshal to `xmm`.** It lowers to visible instructions — inspect with `was … --emit-asm`.

column5: comcall — call a COM method through its vtable
column6: Calls `Method` on the COM object `pObj` via its vtable slot (the `this` pointer is passed first automatically), with the same Win64 marshaling as `invoke`. The slot and parameter types come from the knowledge base. **Prefer the typed sugar:** after `comobj p : ID3D11Device` write `p.CreateRenderTargetView(…)` instead of a raw `comcall`.

column5: comobj — declare a typed COM object pointer
column6: Binds the local pointer `NAME` to a COM `Interface` so that `NAME.Method(args)` resolves the right vtable slot and parameter types. Pure compile-time type info — it emits no code itself.

column5: iid — emit a COM interface GUID
column6: Lays down the 16-byte IID (GUID) of `InterfaceName` as data, for passing to `CoCreateInstance`/`QueryInterface`. The GUID is looked up in the knowledge base.

column5: sizeof — byte size of a type or struct
column6: Resolves at assemble time to the size in bytes of a Windows struct or type, e.g. `sub rsp, sizeof(RECT)`. Field byte offsets use the separate `Struct.field` form.

column4: proc NAME uses r… in … out … [frame] … endproc
column6: Declares a subroutine with a **visible** prologue/epilogue and a checked contract. `uses r12 r13` saves/restores those callee-saved registers; `in`/`out` declare the registers consumed/produced; `frame` reserves the shadow space and alignment **once** so the calls inside go lean. The contract is enforced: a callee-saved register clobbered across a call, an `in`/`out` mismatch, or a stack left off the aligned frame is flagged. **A `proc` that contains `invoke`/`call` must declare `frame`; inside a `frame` proc don't `sub rsp` by hand — use a memory slot or `xmm0–5`. A plain `proc`'s `uses` tracks GP registers only; to preserve the callee-saved vector registers xmm6..xmm15, use `fproc`.**

column4: fproc NAME uses r… xmm… in … out … [frame] … endproc
column5: fproc/endproc — a proc that preserves vector registers too
column6: Like `proc`/`endproc`, but its `uses` may also list the callee-saved vector registers xmm6..xmm15 (ymm/zmm alias the same xmm). The prologue reserves a stack slot and `movups`-saves each; the epilogue reloads them — so float code can keep loop-invariant state in xmm6+ across calls and still honor the Win64 ABI for its own caller. The contract is checked the same way: an xmm clobbered but not in `uses` is flagged — an error in an `fproc` (just add it to `uses`), a warning in a plain `proc` (which cannot save xmm). Reach for `fproc` whenever a subroutine writes xmm6..xmm15.

column5: struct/ends — lay out a struct instance
column6: Reserves and initialises an instance of a Windows struct `TYPE` named `LABEL`, placing fields at the database's byte offsets (`BufferDesc.Width = 1280`). `ends` closes it. Read fields elsewhere with `LABEL.field` / `TYPE.field`.

column4: name BYTE v[, v…]   ·   buf BYTE 64 dup(0)
column6: MASM-style data declarations: `name BYTE 1,2,3`, `msg BYTE "hi",0`, `buf BYTE 64 dup(0)`. `WORD`/`DWORD`/`QWORD` are 2/4/8 bytes; `WCHAR` is a 16-bit UTF-16 unit. **A `dup` count must be a literal (`BYTE 64 dup(0)`, not `BYTE 8*8 dup(0)`).** A bare `','` char literal trips the lexer — use the ASCII number `44` instead.

Stores an IEEE-754 single (`real4`, 4 bytes) or double (`real8`, 8 bytes) from a decimal literal — `freq real8 440.0` lays down the exact bit pattern. Pair it with `invoke`'s `real8 […]` argument annotation to pass a float in `xmm`.

column5: ASCIISTRING/WIDESTRING — embed a raw text block
column6: Emits everything between the markers verbatim as bytes — for embedding an HLSL shader or other multi-line text without escaping. `.WIDESTRING … .ENDWIDESTRING` emits the same as UTF-16.

A high-level conditional that lowers to a `cmp`/`jcc` skeleton you can see in `--emit-asm`. Conditions compare registers/values, picking signed or unsigned tests from the operands. Nest freely; close every `.if` with `.endif`.
Runs the body while `cond` holds, lowering to a visible compare-and-branch. Use `.break`/`.continue` inside; close with `.endw`.
Runs the body at least once, then repeats **until** `cond` becomes true — the inverse-tested sibling of `.while`.
A counted loop over a register from a start to an end value (`.for al = '0' to '9'`), lowering to visible init/compare/increment. `.forever` is an unconditional loop you exit with `.break`.
`.break` exits the innermost `.while`/`.repeat`/`.for`; `.continue` jumps to its next iteration. Conditional forms are also accepted.

Defines a MASM-style macro — `NAME MACRO a, b` … `ENDM` — invoked as `NAME x, y`. `LOCAL lbl` inside gives each expansion unique labels. It expands to visible instructions inline (shown in the ghost listing).

column5: .include — compose a program from many files
column6: Textually includes another source file at this point, so a program can be split across files. Resolved before assembly — and before the IDE's F5 build/run.
Binds `NAME` to a constant value used in place of a literal. In the IDE, Ctrl+click a use to jump to its definition.
Marks `NAME` as global so the linker (and the PE entry point) can see it. `.global` is a synonym.
`.code`/`.text` start the executable code section; `.data` starts the writable data section.
